elements.sent-2411-001: Efficient tokenization of videos remains a challenge in training vision models that can process long videos.
elements.sent-2411-002: One promising direction is to develop a tokenizer that can encode long video clips, as it would enable the tokenizer to leverage the temporal coherence of videos better for tokenization.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, training existing tokenizers on long videos often incurs a huge training cost as they are trained to reconstruct all the frames at once.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we introduce CoordTok, a video tokenizer that learns a mapping from coordinate-based representations to the corresponding patches of input videos, inspired by recent advances in 3D generative models.
elements.sent-2411-005: In particular, CoordTok encodes a video into factorized triplane representations and reconstructs patches that correspond to randomly sampled $(x,y,t)$ coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-006: This allows for training large tokenizer models directly on long videos without requiring excessive training resources.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our experiments show that CoordTok can drastically reduce the number of tokens for encoding long video clips.
elements.sent-2411-008: For instance, CoordTok can encode a 128-frame video with 128$\times$128 resolution into 1280 tokens, while baselines need 6144 or 8192 tokens to achieve similar reconstruction quality.
elements.sent-2411-009: We further show that this efficient video tokenization enables memory-efficient training of a diffusion transformer that can generate 128 frames at once.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{subfigure}{.37\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{assets/training_batch_size_256_v2_.pdf} \caption{Maximum batch-size when training video tokenizers on $128\times128$ resolution videos with varying lengths, measured with a single NVIDIA 4090 24GB GPU.} \label{subfig:long-scalability} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.61\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{assets/motivation_vis_v2.pdf} \caption{Inter-clip reconstruction consistency of video tokenizers. Existing video tokenizers \citep{ge2022long,yu2023video,wang2024larp} show the pixel-value inconsistency between short clips (16 frames). In contrast, Our tokenizer shows the temporally consistent reconstruction.} \label{subfig:inter-clip-inconsistency} \end{subfigure} \caption{Limitation of existing video tokenizers. (a) Existing video tokenizers \citep{ge2022long,yu2023video,wang2024larp} are often not scalable to long videos because of excessive memory and computational demands. This is because they are trained to reconstruct all video frames at once, \ie, a giant 3D array of pixels, which incurs a huge computation and memory burden in training especially when trained on long videos. For instance, PVDM-AE \citep{yu2023video} becomes out-of-memory when trained to encode 128-frame videos when using a single NVIDIA 4090 24GB GPU. (b) As a result, existing tokenizers are typically trained to encode up to 16-frame videos and struggle to capture the temporal coherence of videos.} \label{fig:motivation}
elements.sent-2411-010: Efficient tokenization of videos remains a challenge in developing vision models that can process long videos.
elements.sent-2411-011: While recent video tokenizers have achieved higher compression ratios \citep{ge2022long,blattmann2023align,yu2023magvit,yu2023language,videoworldsimulators2024,wang2024emu3 compared to using image tokenizers for videos (\ie, frame-wise compression) \citep{zhou2024transfusion,team2024chameleon , the vast scale of video data still requires us to design a more efficient video tokenizer.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: One promising direction for efficient video tokenization is enabling video tokenizers to exploit the temporal coherence of videos.
elements.sent-2411-013: For instance, video codecs \citep{rijkse1996h,marpe2006h,sullivan2012overview,mukherjee2015technical extensively utilize such coherence for video compression by extracting keyframes and encoding the difference between them.}
elements.sent-2411-014: In fact, there have been several recent works based on a similar intuition that train a tokenizer to encode videos into factorized representations \citep{yu2023video,kim2024hybrid,yu2024efficient .}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, a key limitation is that existing tokenizers are typically trained to encode short video clips because of high training cost, but it is more likely that tokenizers can better exploit the temporal coherence when they are trained on longer videos.
elements.sent-2411-016: For instance, because tokenizers are trained to reconstruct all the frames at once, their training cost increases linearly with the length of videos (see \Cref{subfig:long-scalability ).}
elements.sent-2411-017: This makes it difficult to train tokenizers that can encode long videos and thus capture the temporal coherence of videos (see \Cref{subfig:inter-clip-inconsistency ).}
elements.sent-2411-018: In this paper, we aim to design a video tokenizer that can be easily scaled up to encode long videos.
elements.sent-2411-019: To this end, we draw inspiration from recent works that have successfully trained large 3D generative models in a compute-efficient manner \citep{hong2023lrm,jun2023shap,miyato2023gta,liu2024one .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Their key idea is to train a model that learns a mapping from randomly sampled $(x, y, z)$ coordinates to RGB and density values instead of training with all the possible coordinates at once.
elements.sent-2411-021: In particular, we ask: can we utilize a similar idea to design a scalable video tokenizer? Actually, there have been recent studies that formulate the video reconstruction as a problem of learning the mapping from $(x, y, t)$ coordinates to RGB values \citep{kim2022scalable,chen2022videoinr .}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, they rather focus on compressing each individual video instead of training a video tokenizer that can encode a diverse set of videos.
elements.sent-2411-023: We introduce CoordTok: Coordinate-based patch reconstruction for long video Tokenization, a scalable video tokenizer that learns a mapping from coordinate-based representations to the corresponding patches of input videos.
elements.sent-2411-024: The key idea of CoordTok is to encode a video into factorized triplane representations \citep{kim2022scalable,yu2023video and reconstruct patches that correspond to randomly sampled $(x,y,t)$ coordinates (see \Cref{fig:method_overview ).}}
elements.sent-2411-025: This enables the training of large tokenizers directly on long videos without excessive memory and computational requirements (see \Cref{subfig:long-scalability ).}
elements.sent-2411-026: To investigate whether training a video tokenizer on long video clips indeed leads to more efficient tokenization, we compare CoordTok with other baselines \citep{ge2022long,yu2023video,yu2023magvit,wang2024larp on the UCF-101 dataset \citep{soomro2012ucf101 .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Our experiments show that, by exploiting the temporal coherence of videos, CoordTok significantly reduces the number of tokens for encoding long videos compared to baselines.
elements.sent-2411-028: For instance, CoordTok encodes a 128-frame video with 128$\times$128 resolution into only 1280 tokens, while baselines require 6144 or 8192 tokens to achieve similar encoding quality.
elements.sent-2411-029: We also show that efficient tokenization with CoordTok enables memory-efficient training of a diffusion transformer \citep{peebles2023scalable,ma2024sit that can generate a 128-frame video at once.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Finally, we provide an extensive analysis on the effect of various design choices.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.99\linewidth]{assets/method_overview.pdf} \caption{Overview of CoordTok. We design our encoder to encode a video $\rvx$ into factorized triplane representations $\rvz = [\rvz^{xy}, \rvz^{yt}, \rvz^{xt}]$ which can efficiently represent the video with three 2D latent planes. Given the triplane representations $\mathbf{z}$, our decoder learns a mapping from $(x,y,t)$ coordinates to RGB pixels within the corresponding patches. In particular, we extract coordinate-based representations of $N$ sampled coordinates by querying the coordinates from triplane representations via bilinear interpolation. Then the decoder aggregates and fuses information from different coordinates with self-attention layers and project outputs into corresponding patches. This design enables us to train tokenizers on long videos in a compute-efficient manner by avoiding reconstruction of entire frames at once. } \label{fig:method_overview}
elements.sent-2411-031: We summarize the contributions of this paper below:.
elements.sent-2411-032: We introduce CoordTok, a scalable video tokenizer that learns a mapping from coordinate-based representations to the corresponding patches of input videos.
elements.sent-2411-033: We show that CoordTok can leverage the temporal coherence of videos for tokenization, drastically reducing the number of tokens required for encoding long videos.
elements.sent-2411-034: We show that efficient video tokenization with CoordTok enables memory-efficient training of a diffusion transformer \citep{peebles2023scalable,ma2024sit that can generate long videos at once.}
elements.sent-2411-035: In this section, we present CoordTok, a scalable video tokenizer that can efficiently encode long videos.
elements.sent-2411-036: In a nutshell, CoordTok encodes a video into factorized triplane representations \citep{kim2022scalable,yu2023video and learns a mapping from randomly sampled $(x, y, t)$ coordinates to pixels from the corresponding patches.}
elements.sent-2411-037: We provide the overview of CoordTok in \Cref{fig:method_overview .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Let $\rvx$ be a video and $\mathcal{D}$ be a dataset consisting of videos.
elements.sent-2411-039: Our goal is to train a video tokenizer that encodes a video $\rvx \in \mathcal{D}$ into tokens (or a low-dimensional latent vector) $\rvz$ and decodes $\rvz$ into $\rvx$.
elements.sent-2411-040: In particular, we want the tokenizer to be efficient so that it can encode videos into fewer number of tokens as possible but still can decode tokens to the original video $\rvx$ without loss of information.
elements.sent-2411-041: Given a video $\rvx$, we divide the video into non-overlapping space-time patches.
elements.sent-2411-042: We then add learnable positional embeddings and process them through a series of transformer layers \citep{vaswani2017attention to obtain video features $\rve$.}
elements.sent-2411-043: We further encode video features $\rve$ into factorized triplane representations \citep{chan2022efficient,yu2023video , \ie, $\rvz = [\rvz^{xy , \rvz^{yt , \rvz^{xt ]$, where $\rvz^{xy}$ captures the global content in $\rvx$ across time (\eg, layout and appearance of the scene or object), $\rvz^{yt}$ and $\rvz^{xt}$ capture the underlying motion in $\rvx$ across two spatial axes (see \Cref{fig:latent-visualization for visualization).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: This design is efficient because it represents a video with three 2D latent planes instead of 3D latents widely used in prior approaches \citep{ge2022long,yu2023magvit,wang2024emu3 .}
elements.sent-2411-045: We implement our encoder based on the memory-efficient design of a recent 3D generation work \citep{hong2023lrm that introduces learnable embeddings and translates them to triplane representations.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Specifically, we first introduce learnable embeddings $\rvz_{0 = [\rvz_{0 ^{xy , \rvz_{0 ^{yt , \rvz_{0 ^{xt ]$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: We then process them through a series of cross-self attention layers, where each layer consists of (i) cross-attention layer that attends to the video features $\rve$ and (ii) self-attention layer that attends to its own features.
elements.sent-2411-048: In practice, we split each learnable embedding into four smaller equal-sized embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-049: We then use them as inputs to the cross-self encoder, because we find it helps the model to use more computation by increasing the length of input sequence.
elements.sent-2411-050: Finally, we project the outputs into triplane representations to obtain $\rvz = [\rvz^{xy , \rvz^{yt , \rvz^{xt ]$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Given the triplane representation $\rvz = [\rvz^{xy , \rvz^{yt , \rvz^{xt ]$, we implement our decoder to learn a mapping from $(x, y, t)$ coordinate to the pixels of corresponding patch.}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: We first divide the video into non-overlapping space-time patches.
elements.sent-2411-053: We note that the configuration of patches, \eg, patch sizes, may differ from the one used in the video encoder.
elements.sent-2411-054: We then randomly sample $(x, y, t)$ coordinates by sampling $N$ patches and extracting the center coordinates of sampled patches.
elements.sent-2411-055: We find that sampling only $3$\% of video patches can achieve strong performance (see \Cref{tab:analysis-partial-recon for the effect of sampling).}
elements.sent-2411-056: As inputs to the decoder, we use coordinate-based representations $\rvh$ that are obtained by querying each coordinate from triplane representations via bilinear interpolation.
elements.sent-2411-057: Specifically, let $(i, j, k)$ be one of sampled coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-058: We extract $\rvh^{xy}$ by querying $(i, j)$ from $\rvz^{xy}$, $\rvh^{yt}$ by querying $(j, k)$ from $\rvz^{yt}$, and $\rvh^{xt}$ by querying $(i, k)$ from $\rvz^{xt}$.
elements.sent-2411-059: We concatenate them to get coordinate-based representations $\rvh$.
elements.sent-2411-060: Given $N$ coordinate-based representations $[\rvh_{1 , ..., \rvh_{N ]$, our decoder processes them through a series of self-attention layers, enabling each $\rvh_{i}$ to attend to other representations $\rvh_{j}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: This allows the decoder to aggregate and fuse the information from different coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-062: We then use a linear projection layer to process the output from each $\rvh_{i}$ to pixels of the corresponding patch.
elements.sent-2411-063: Finally, we update the parameters of our encoder and decoder to minimize an $\ell_{2}$ loss between the reconstructed pixels and original pixels.
elements.sent-2411-064: To further improve the quality of reconstructed videos, we introduce an additional fine-tuning phase where we train our tokenizer with both $\ell_{2}$ loss and LPIPS loss \citep{zhang2018unreasonable .}
elements.sent-2411-065: Specifically, instead of sampling coordinates, we randomly sample a few frames and use all coordinates within the sampled frames for fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-066: This enables the tokenizer to compute and minimize LPIPS loss, which requires reconstructing the entire frame.
elements.sent-2411-067: While we find that sampling frames instead of coordinates from the beginning of the training is harmful due to the lack of diversity in training data (see \Cref{tab:analysis-partial-recon ), we find that fine-tuning with sampled frames improves the quality of reconstructed videos.}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{assets/qualitative_results_recon_v3.pdf} \caption{128-frame, 128$\times$128 resolution video reconstruction results from \sname (Ours) and baselines \citep{yu2023video,wang2024larp} trained on the UCF-101 dataset \citep{soomro2012ucf101}. For each frame, we visualize the ground-truth (GT) and reconstructed pixels within the region highlighted in the red box, where CoordTok achieves noticeably better reconstruction quality than other baselines. } \label{fig:qualitative_results}
elements.sent-2411-068: We design experiments to investigate following questions:.
elements.sent-2411-069: Can \sname efficiently encode long videos? Does encoding long videos lead to efficient video tokenization? (\Cref{fig:qualitative_results,fig:recon_vs_token,tab:main ).}
elements.sent-2411-070: Can \sname learn meaningful tokens that can be used for downstream tasks such as video generation? (\Cref{fig:qualitative_results_gen,tab:long-video-generation ) Can efficient video tokenization improve video generation models? (\Cref{tab:long-video-generation-efficiency,fig:generation_fvd_curve ).}}
elements.sent-2411-071: What is the effect of various design choices? (\Cref{fig:analysis-scalability,tab:analysis-partial-recon ).}
elements.sent-2411-072: We conduct all our experiments on the UCF-101 \citep{soomro2012ucf101 dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-073: Following the setup of prior works \citep{ge2022long,yu2023magvit , we use the train split of the UCF-101 dataset for training.}
elements.sent-2411-074: For preprocessing videos, we resize and center-crop the frames to $128\times128$ resolution.
elements.sent-2411-075: We train our tokenizer using the AdamW optimizer \citep{loshchilov2017decoupled with a batch size of 256, where each sample is a randomly sampled 128-frame video.}
elements.sent-2411-076: We use $N=1024$ coordinates for main training and $N=4096$ for fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-077: For the main experimental results, we train CoordTok for 1M iterations and further fine-tune it with LPIPS loss for 50k iterations.
elements.sent-2411-078: For analysis and ablation studies, we train CoordTok for 200k iterations and further fine-tune it with LPIPS loss for 10k iterations.
elements.sent-2411-079: For model configurations such as embedding dimension and number of layers, we mostly follow the architectures of vision transformers (ViTs; \citep{dosovitskiy2020image ).}
elements.sent-2411-080: We provide more detailed implementation details in \cref{appendix:implementation_details .}

elements.sent-2411-103: To investigate whether CoordTok can encode long videos into meaningful tokens, we consider an unconditional video generation setup where we train a model to produce 128-frame videos.
elements.sent-2411-104: Videos of length 128 are often considered too long to be generated at once, so several works use techniques such as iterative generation \citep{yu2023video for generating long videos.}

elements.sent-2411-141: Many recent works have explored the idea of using video tokenizers to encode videos into low-dimensional latent tokens.
elements.sent-2411-142: Initial attempts proposed to directly use image tokenizers for videos \citep{van2017neural,razavi2019generating,esser2021taming via frame-wise compression.}
elements.sent-2411-143: However, this approach overlooks the temporal coherence of videos, resulting in inefficient compression.
elements.sent-2411-144: Thus, recent works have proposed to train a tokenizer specialized for videos \citep{yan2021videogpt,ge2022long,hong2022cogvideo,blattmann2023align,yu2023magvit,yu2023language,yan2023temporally,yang2024cogvideox,videoworldsimulators2024,wang2024emu3,wang2024omnitokenizer .}
elements.sent-2411-145: They typically extend image tokenizers by replacing spatial layers with spatiotemporal layers (\eg, 2D convolutional layers to 3D convolution layers).
elements.sent-2411-146: More recent works have introduced efficient tokenization schemes with careful consideration of redundancy in video data.
elements.sent-2411-147: For instance, several works proposed to encode videos into factorized triplane representations \citep{yu2023video,kim2024hybrid,yu2024efficient , and another line of works proposed an adaptive encoding scheme that utilizes the redundancy of videos for tokenization \citep{yan2024elastictok,wang2024larp .}}
elements.sent-2411-148: However, they still train the tokenizer through reconstruction of entire video frames, so training is only possible with short video clips split from the original long videos.
elements.sent-2411-149: Our work introduces a video tokenizer that can directly handle much longer video clips by removing the need for a decoder to reconstruct entire video frames during training.
elements.sent-2411-150: By capturing the global information present in long videos, we show that our tokenizer achieves more effective tokenization.
elements.sent-2411-151: Instead of modeling distributions of complex and high-dimensional video pixels, most recent video generation models focus on learning the latent distribution induced by video tokenizers, as it can dramatically reduce memory and computation bottlenecks.
elements.sent-2411-152: One approach involves training autoregressive models \citep{yan2021videogpt,kondratyuk2023videopoet,ge2022long in a discrete token space \citep{van2017neural,razavi2019generating .}}
elements.sent-2411-153: Another line of research \citep{villegas2022phenaki,yan2023temporally, yoo2023towards also considers discrete latent space but has trained masked generative transformer (MaskGiT; \citep{chang2022maskgit ) for generative modeling.}}
elements.sent-2411-154: Finally, many recent works \citep{blattmann2023align,gupta2023photorealistic,yu2023video,kim2024hybrid,he2022lvdm,lu2023vdt,singer2022make,zhou2022magicvideo have trained diffusion models \citep{sohl2015deep,ho2020denoising in continuous latent space, inspired by the success of latent diffusion models in the image domain \citep{rombach2022high .}}}
elements.sent-2411-155: Despite their efforts, the models are typically limited to processing only short video clips at a time (usually 16-frame clips), which makes it difficult for the model to generate longer videos.
elements.sent-2411-156: In this paper, we significantly improve the limited contextual length of latent video generation models by introducing an efficient video tokenizer.
elements.sent-2411-157: In this paper, we have presented CoordTok, a scalable video tokenizer that learns a mapping from coordinate-based representations to the corresponding patches of input videos.
elements.sent-2411-158: CoordTok is built upon our intuition that training a tokenizer directly on long videos would enable the tokenizer to leverage the temporal coherence of videos for efficient tokenization.
elements.sent-2411-159: Our experiments show that CoordTok can encode long videos using far fewer number of tokens than existing baselines.
elements.sent-2411-160: We also find that this efficient video tokenization enables memory-efficient training of video generation models that can generate long videos at once.
elements.sent-2411-161: We hope that our work further facilitates future researches on designing scalable video tokenizers and efficient video generation models.
elements.sent-2411-162: One limitation of our work is that our tokenizer struggles more with dynamic videos than with static videos, as shown in \cref{fig:recon_analysis .}
elements.sent-2411-163: We hypothesize this is due to the difficulty of learning to decompose dynamic videos into global content and motion.
elements.sent-2411-164: One interesting future direction could involve introducing multiple content planes across the temporal dimension.
elements.sent-2411-165: Moreover, future work may introduce an adaptive method for deciding the number of such content planes based on how dynamic each video is, similar to techniques in video codecs \citep{wiegand2003overview,sze2014high,mukherjee2015technical,han2021technical or an adaptive encoding scheme designed for a recent video tokenizer \citep{yan2024elastictok .}}
elements.sent-2411-166: Lastly, we are excited about scaling up our tokenizer to longer videos from larger datasets and evaluating it on challenging downstream tasks such as long video understanding and generation.
elements.sent-2411-167: We train \sname via AdamW optimizer \citep{loshchilov2017decoupled with a constant learning rate of $10^{-4}$, $(\beta_1, \beta_2)=(0.9, 0.999)$, and weight decay $0.001$.}
elements.sent-2411-168: We use a batch size of 256, where each sample is a randomly sampled 128-frame video.
elements.sent-2411-169: In the main training stage, we reconstruct $N=1024$ randomly sampled coordinates and update the model using $\ell_2$ loss.
elements.sent-2411-170: In the fine-tuning stage, we reconstruct 16 randomly sampled frames (i.e., $N=4096$ coordinates) and update the model using a combination of $\ell_2$ loss and LPIPS loss with equal weights.
elements.sent-2411-171: To speed up training, we use mixed-precision (fp16).
elements.sent-2411-172: For the main experimental results, we train \sname for 1M iterations and fine-tune it for 50K iterations.
elements.sent-2411-173: For analysis and ablation studies, we train \sname for 200K iterations and fine-tune it for 10K iterations.
elements.sent-2411-174: CoordTok consists of a transformer encoder that extracts video features from raw videos, a cross-self encoder that processes video features into triplane representations via cross-attention between learnable parameters and video features, and a transformer decoder that learns a mapping from coordinate-based representations into corresponding patches.
elements.sent-2411-175: In what follows, we describe each component in detail.
elements.sent-2411-176: Transformer encoder consists of a Conv3D patch embedding, learnable positional embedding, and transformer layers, where each transformer layer comprises self-attention and feed-forward layers.
elements.sent-2411-177: Cross-self encoder consists of plane-wise Conv2D patch embeddings, transformer layers, and plane-wise linear projectors, where each transformer layer comprises cross-attention, self-attention, and feed-forward layers.
elements.sent-2411-178: Transformer decoder consists of linear patch embedding, learnable positional embedding, transformer layers, and a linear projector, where each transformer layer comprises self-attention and feed-forward layers.
elements.sent-2411-179: We provide the detailed architecture configurations for each model size in \Cref{tab:model-config .}

elements.paragraph-2411-016: Detailed description of coordinate-based patch reconstruction
elements.sent-2411-180: We describe the coordinate-based patch reconstruction process of \sname during training, which involves: (1) constructing the inputs and target outputs for the decoder, (2) obtaining coordinate-based representations from the inputs, and (3) reconstructing the target outputs using these representations.
elements.sent-2411-182: Given a video $\rvx$, we patchify it and set $N$ patch coordinates as inputs and their corresponding patch values as target outputs.
elements.sent-2411-183: Formally, we start by patchifying the video $\rvx$, \ie, $Patchify(\rvx) := \{(m, \rvx_{(m) )\}_{m=1 ^M$.}}
elements.sent-2411-184: We then convert each patch index $m$ into the coordinates $(i,j,k)$ representing the center position of the patch along each axis relative to the entire video $\rvx$, \ie, $i,j,k \in [0, 1]$.
elements.sent-2411-186: We note that we use entire $M$ patches for evaluation. \begin{equation} Decoder input: [(i_1, j_1, k_1), \cdots, (i_N, j_N, k_N)] Target output: [\rvx_{(i_1j_1k_1)}, \cdots, \rvx_{(i_Nj_Nk_N)}] \label{eq:decoder_input_output} \end{equation} We also use triplane representation $\rvz = [\rvz^{xy , \rvz^{yt , \rvz^{xt ]$ as input of decoder, where the planes have the shape of $H' \times W'$, $W' \times T'$, and $H' \times T'$, respectively.}}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation} Decoder input: [(i_1, j_1, k_1), \cdots, (i_N, j_N, k_N)] Target output: [\rvx_{(i_1j_1k_1)}, \cdots, \rvx_{(i_Nj_Nk_N)}] \label{eq:decoder_input_output} \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-187: We use coordinate-based representations $[\rvh_1, \cdots, \rvh_N]$ obtained by triplane representation as input of the transformer decoder.
elements.sent-2411-188: Formally, let $(i,j,k)\in[0,1]^3$ be one of the sampled input coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-189: We extract $\rvh^{xy}$ by querying $(i,j)$ from $\rvz^{xy}$, $\rvh^{yt}$ by querying $(j,k)$ from $\rvz^{yt}$, and $\rvh^{xt}$ by querying $(i,k)$ from $\rvz^{xt}$ as follows: \begin{equation} \rvh^{xy} = Bilinear((i,j); \rvz^{xy}_{lm}, \rvz^{xy}_{l,m+1},\rvz^{xy}_{l+1,m},\rvz^{xy}_{l+1,m+1}) \rvh^{yt} = Bilinear((j,k); \rvz^{yt}_{mn}, \rvz^{yt}_{m,n+1},\rvz^{yt}_{m+1,n},\rvz^{yt}_{m+1,n+1}) \rvh^{xt} = Bilinear((i,k); \rvz^{xt}_{ln}, \rvz^{xt}_{l,n+1},\rvz^{xt}_{l+1,n},\rvz^{xt}_{l+1,n+1}) \label{eq:bilinear_interpolation} \end{equation} where $(l,m,n) = (\lfloor i\times(H'-1) \rfloor, \lfloor j\times(W'-1) \rfloor, \lfloor k\times(T'-1) \rfloor)$, $(\rvz^{xy _{lm , \rvz^{yt _{mn , \rvz^{xt _{ln )$ indicates the latent vector in $\rvz$ at coordinates $(l,m,n)$, and $\text{Bilinear (\cdot;\cdot)$ indicates the bilinear interpolation operation at the input coordinate between given vectors.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-190: We then concatenate them to get coordinate-based representation of $(i,j,k)$, \ie, $\rvh:=\text{Concat (\rvh^{xy , \rvh^{yt , \rvh^{xt )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-191: Given $N$ coordinate-based representations $[\rvh_1, \cdots, \rvh_N]$, the transformer decoder processes them through self-attention layers to reconstruct the patches $[\hat{\rvx _{(i_1j_1k_1) , \cdots, \hat{\rvx _{(i_Nj_Nk_N) ]$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-192: Before the transformer layers, we add positional embeddings corresponding to the coordinates $(i_1, j_1, k_1), \cdots, (i_N, j_N, k_N)$ to each patch.
elements.sent-2411-193: This allows the self-attention layers to effectively distinguish each patch.
elements.sent-2411-194: We implement CoordTok-SiT-L/2 based on the original SiT implementation \citep{ma2024sit .}
elements.sent-2411-195: The inputs of SiT-L/2 are the normalized triplane representation obtained by tokenizing video clips of length 128 with CoordTok.
elements.sent-2411-196: To normalize the triplane representation, we randomly sample 2048 video clips of length 128 and calculate the mean and standard deviation for each plane.
elements.sent-2411-197: We train SiT-L/2 via AdamW optimizer \citep{loshchilov2017decoupled with a constant learning rate of $10^{-4}$, $(\beta_1, \beta_2)=(0.9, 0.999)$, and no weight decay.}
elements.sent-2411-198: We use a batch size of 64.
elements.sent-2411-199: We train the model for 600K iterations and we update an EMA model with a momentum parameter 0.9999.
elements.sent-2411-200: We use the same structure as SiT, except that our patch embedding and final projection layers are implemented separately for each plane.
elements.sent-2411-201: To train the unconditional video generation model, we assume the number of classes as 1, and we set the class dropout ratio to 0.

elements.sent-2411-205: For our \sname, we tokenize and reconstruct 128-frame videos all at once.
elements.sent-2411-206: Specifically, we encode the video into a triplane representation and then reconstruct the video by passing all patch coordinates through the transformer decoder at once.
elements.sent-2411-207: In contrast, the baselines can only handle videos of much shorter lengths (e.g., 16 frames for PVDM-AE \citep{yu2023video ).}
elements.sent-2411-208: Therefore, to evaluate the reconstruction quality of 128-frame videos for the baselines, we split the videos into short clips and tokenize and reconstruct them.
elements.sent-2411-209: To be specific, we first split a 128-frame video into shorter clips suitable for each tokenizer.
elements.sent-2411-210: We then tokenize and reconstruct each short clip individually using the tokenizer.
elements.sent-2411-211: Finally, we concatenate all the reconstructed short clips to obtain the 128-frame video.
elements.sent-2411-212: For evaluating the quality of reconstructed videos, we follow the setup of MAGVIT \citep{yu2023magvit .}
elements.sent-2411-213: We randomly sample 10000 video clips of length 128, and then measure the reconstruction quality using the metrics as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-214: It uses the I3D network \citep{carreira2017quo to extract features, and it computes the distance based on the assumption that both feature distributions are multivariate Gaussian.}
elements.sent-2411-215: Specifically, we compute the rFVD score on video clips of length 128.
elements.sent-2411-216: PSNR measures the similarity between pixel values of real and reconstructed images using the mean squared error.
elements.sent-2411-217: For videos, we compute the PSNR score for each frame and then average these frame-wise PSNR scores.
elements.sent-2411-218: LPIPS \citep{zhang2018unreasonable measures the perceptual similarity between real and reconstructed images by computing the feature distance using a pre-trained VGG network \citep{simonyan2014very .}}
elements.sent-2411-219: It aggregates the distance of features extracted from various layers.
elements.sent-2411-220: For videos, we compute the LPIPS score for each frame and then average these frame-wise LPIPS scores.
elements.sent-2411-221: SSIM \citep{wang2004image measures the structural similarity between real and reconstructed images by comparing luminance, contrast, and structural information.}
elements.sent-2411-222: For videos, we compute the SSIM score for each frame and then average these frame-wise SSIM scores.
elements.sent-2411-223: For sname-SiT-L/2, we generate the tokens corresponding to a 128-frame video all at once and then decode these tokens using \sname.
elements.sent-2411-224: In contrast, baselines iteratively generate 128-frame videos.
elements.sent-2411-225: For instance, PVDM and HVDM generate the next 16-frame video conditioned on the previously generated 16-frame video clip.
elements.sent-2411-226: For evaluating the quality of generated videos, we strictly follow the setup of StyleGAN-V \citep{skorokhodov2022stylegan that calculates the FVD scores \citep{unterthiner2019fvd between the distribution of real and generated videos.}}
elements.sent-2411-227: To be specific, we use 2048 video clips of length 128 for each distribution, where the real videos are sampled from the dataset used to train generation models (\ie, the UCF-101 dataset \citep{soomro2012ucf101 ).}
elements.sent-2411-228: To measure how dynamic each video is, we use the pixel value differences between consecutive frames.
elements.sent-2411-229: To be specific, we compute the dynamics magnitude for each pair of consecutive frames, calculate the mean of these values, and then take the logarithm.
elements.sent-2411-230: Here, dynamics magnitude of two frames $f^1$ and $f^2$ of resolution $H \times W$ can be defined as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:frame_distance} d(f^1, f^2) ={\frac{1}{HW}} \sum_{h=1}^H \sum_{w=1}^W d_{2} (f^1_{hw}, f^2_{hw}),\end{equation} where $f^i_{hw}$ denotes the RGB values at coordinates $(h, w)$ of frame $f^i$ and $d_2$ denotes $\ell_2$-distance of RGB pixel values.
elements.sent-2411-231: In \Cref{subfig:recon_frame_distance , we standardize the video dynamics score into a range of 0 to 100.}
elements.sent-2411-232: To measure the frequency magnitude, we use the metric proposed in \citet{yan2024elastictok that utilizes a Sobel edge detection filter.}
elements.sent-2411-233: To be specific, to get the frequency magnitude, we apply both horizontal and vertical Sobel filters to each frame to compute the gradient magnitude at each pixel.
elements.sent-2411-234: We then calculate the average of these magnitudes across all pixels.

elements.figure-2411-018: \begin{subfigure}{.37\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{assets/training_batch_size_256_v4.pdf} \caption{Maximum batch-size when training video tokenizers on 128$\times$128 resolution videos with varying lengths, measured with a single NVIDIA 4090 24GB GPU.} \label{subfig:long-scalability} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.61\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{assets/motivation_vis_v2.pdf} \caption{Inter-clip reconstruction consistency of video tokenizers. Existing video tokenizers \citep{ge2022long,yu2023video,wang2024larp} show the pixel-value inconsistency between short clips (16 frames). In contrast, Our tokenizer shows the temporally consistent reconstruction.} \label{subfig:inter-clip-inconsistency} \end{subfigure} \caption{Limitation of existing video tokenizers. (a) Existing video tokenizers \citep{ge2022long,yu2023video,wang2024larp} are often not scalable to long videos because of excessive memory and computational demands. This is because they are trained to reconstruct all video frames at once, \ie, a giant 3D array of pixels, which incurs a huge computation and memory burden in training especially when trained on long videos. For instance, PVDM-AE \citep{yu2023video} becomes out-of-memory when trained to encode 128-frame videos when using a single NVIDIA 4090 24GB GPU. (b) As a result, existing tokenizers are typically trained to encode up to 16-frame videos and struggle to capture the temporal coherence of videos.} \label{fig:motivation}
elements.sent-2411-256: After that, we encode video features $\rve$ into factorized triplane representations \citep{chan2022efficient,yu2023video , \ie, $\rvz = [\rvz^{xy , \rvz^{yt , \rvz^{xt ]$, where the planes have the shape of $H' \times W'$, $W' \times T'$, and $H' \times T'$, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-257: Intuitively, $\rvz^{xy}$ captures the global content in $\rvx$ across time (\eg, layout and appearance of the scene or object), $\rvz^{yt}$ and $\rvz^{xt}$ capture the underlying motion in $\rvx$ across two spatial axes (see \Cref{fig:latent-visualization for visualization).}
elements.sent-2411-258: Given the triplane representation $\rvz = [\rvz^{xy , \rvz^{yt , \rvz^{xt ]$, we implement our decoder to reconstruct partial video during the training stage by learning a mapping from $(i,j,k)$ coordinate to the pixels of the corresponding patch.}}}
elements.sent-2411-259: We use patch coordinates as inputs to the decoder and their corresponding patch RGB values as targets.
elements.sent-2411-260: Specifically, we first divide the video $\rvx$ into non-overlapping space-time patches.
elements.sent-2411-261: We then convert each patch index into the $(i,j,k)$ coordinates representing the center position of the patch along each $x$, $y$, and $t$ axis relative to the entire video $\rvx$, where $i,j,k \in [0, 1]$.
elements.sent-2411-262: We find that sampling only $3$\% of video patches can achieve strong performance (see \Cref{tab:analysis-partial-recon for the effect of sampling). \begin{equation} Input: [(i_1, j_1, k_1), \cdots, (i_N, j_N, k_N)] Target: [\rvx_{i_1 j_1 k_1}, \cdots, \rvx_{i_N j_N k_N}] \label{eq:decoder_input_output} \end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-263: As inputs to the transformer decoder, we use coordinate-based representations $\rvh$ that are obtained by querying each input coordinate from triplane representation via bilinear interpolation.
elements.sent-2411-264: More specifically, let $(l, m, n)$ be the indices in the triplane representation corresponding to $(i, j, k)$, obtained using the floor function, \ie, $(l,m,n) = (\lfloor i H' \rfloor, \lfloor jW' \rfloor, \lfloor k T' \rfloor)$.
elements.sent-2411-265: Then, coordinate-based representations are computed as follows: \begin{adjustwidth {-0.15cm {0pt \begin{equation} \rvh^{xy} = Bilerp((i,j); \rvz^{xy}_{lm}, \rvz^{xy}_{l,m+1},\rvz^{xy}_{l+1,m},\rvz^{xy}_{l+1,m+1}) \rvh^{yt} = Bilerp((j,k); \rvz^{yt}_{mn}, \rvz^{yt}_{m,n+1},\rvz^{yt}_{m+1,n},\rvz^{yt}_{m+1,n+1}) \rvh^{xt} = Bilerp((i,k); \rvz^{xt}_{ln}, \rvz^{xt}_{l,n+1},\rvz^{xt}_{l+1,n},\rvz^{xt}_{l+1,n+1}) \label{eq:bilinear_interpolation} \end{equation} \end{adjustwidth where $(\rvz^{xy _{lm , \rvz^{yt _{mn , \rvz^{xt _{ln )$ indicates the latent vector in $\rvz$ at indices $(l,m,n)$, and $\text{Bilerp (\cdot;\cdot)$ is the bilinear interpolation operation at the input coordinate between given vectors.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-266: We then concatenate them to get the coordinate-based representation of $(i,j,k)$, \ie, $\rvh:=\text{Concat (\rvh^{xy , \rvh^{yt , \rvh^{xt )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-267: Given $N$ coordinate-based representations $[\rvh_{1 , ..., \rvh_{N ]$, our decoder processes them through a series of self-attention layers, enabling each $\rvh_{n}$ to attend to other representations $\rvh_{m}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-268: We then use a linear projection layer to process the output from each $\rvh_{n}$ to pixels of the corresponding patch $\rvx_{i_n j_n k_n}$.

elements.figure-2411-023: \begin{subfigure}{.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{assets/analysis_pixel_l2_gray.pdf} \caption{{Effect of triplane representations.}} \label{subfig:recon_frame_distance} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{assets/analysis_frequency_mean.pdf} \caption{{Effect of coordinate-based representations}} \label{subfig:recon_frequency} \end{subfigure} \caption{ Analysis on the effect of (a) triplane representations and (b) coordinate-based representations. (a) We measure the Pearson correlation $r$ between the reconstruction quality and a dynamics metric that measures how dynamic each video is. A video with a larger dynamics magnitude indicates a more dynamic video. We find that the correlation is stronger for CoordTok compared to TATS-AE \citep{ge2022long} and MaskGIT-AE \citep{chang2022maskgit}, which encode videos into 3D latents. We hypothesize this is because it is difficult to decompose dynamic videos into contents ($\rvz^{xy}$) and motions ($\rvz^{yt}$, $\rvz^{xt}$). (b) We measure the Pearson correlation $r$ between the reconstruction quality and a frequency metric that measures the fineness of video details \citep{yan2024elastictok}. A video with a larger frequency magnitude indicates a finer-grained video. In this case, we find that the correlation is weaker for CoordTok compared to other tokenizers. We hypothesize this is because CoordTok explicitly learns a mapping from each coordinate-based representation to pixels within the corresponding patch. } \label{fig:recon_analysis}
